revision.comment: /* Use and history */ disambiguating the two sutras
revision.text:
{{Short description|Mantra in Japanese Buddhism}} {{Use dmy dates|date=August 2019}} The '''Mantra of Light''' ([[Japanese language|Japanese]]: ''kōmyō shingon'', 光明真言, Sanskrit: ''Prabhāsa-mantra''), also called the ''Mantra of the Light of Great Consecration'' (Ch: 大灌頂光真言) and ''Mantra of the Unfailing Rope Snare'', is an important [[mantra]] of the [[Shingon]] and [[Kegon]] sects of [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]]. It is also recited in Japanese [[Zen|Zen Buddhism]].Unno, Mark, ''Shingon Refractions: Myoe and the Mantra of Light,'' Ch. 1. The mantra is found in the ''Amoghapāśa-kalparāja-sūtra'' (Chinese translation at [[Taishō Tripiṭaka|Taisho]] no. 1092) as well as in the ''Sutra of the Mantra of the Unfailing Rope Snare of the Buddha Vairocana's Great Baptism'' (不空羂索毘盧遮那佛大灌頂光真言一卷, Taisho no. 1002) and is associated with the deity Amoghapāśa (lit. "Unfailing Rope"), a form of [[Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteshvara]]. It is also the mantra associated with the consecration (abhiseka) of Amoghapāśa by myriad Buddhas (hence its name as "mantra for the mudrā consecration" in the Tibetan version of the text)''.''{{cite book | last = Unno | first = Mark | title = Shingon Refractions: Myoe and the Mantra of Light | publisher = Wisdom Press | year = 2004 | isbn = 0-86171-390-7 | pages=1, 26–41}}{{Cite web |title=The Sovereign Ritual of Amoghapāśa {{!}} 84000 Reading Room |url=https://read.84000.co/translation/toh686.html?id=&part= |access-date=2022-12-06 |website=84000 Translating The Words of The Buddha |language=en}} == Mantra == [[file:Niutsuhimejinja 09.JPG|thumb|Stone stele of the Mantra of Light, Niutsuhime-jinja, Japan]] The "mantra of light" is the following: '''[[Sanskrit]]''' *Roman Script: Oṃ Amogha [[Vairocana]] [[Mahamudra|Mahāmudrā]] Maṇipadma Jvālapravarttaya Hūṃ *[[Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ]]: {{Script|Sidd|𑖌𑖼𑖀𑖦𑖺𑖑𑖪𑖹𑖨𑖺𑖓𑖡𑖦𑖮𑖯𑖦𑖲𑖟𑖿𑖨𑖯𑖦𑖜𑖰𑖢𑖟𑖿𑖦𑖕𑖿𑖪𑖯𑖩𑖢𑖿𑖨𑖪𑖨𑖿𑖝𑖿𑖝𑖧𑖮𑗝𑖽}} *[[Devanāgarī|Devanagari]]: ॐ अमोघ वैरोचन महामुद्रा मणिपद्म ज्वाल प्रवर्त्तय हूँ *{{Audio|Mantra_of_light.OGG|Sanskrit ''Pronunciation''}} '''[[Chinese language|Chinese script]]''' * 唵 阿謨伽 尾盧左曩 摩訶母捺囉 麽抳 鉢納麽 入嚩攞 鉢囉韈哆野 吽 * Ǎn ā mó jiā wěi lú zuǒ nǎng mó hē mǔ nà luō me nǐ bō nà me rù mó luó bō luō wà duō yě hōng '''[[Japanese language|Japanese]]''' * おん あぼきゃ べいろしゃのう まかぼだら まに はんどま じんばら はらばりたや うん * Romanized: Om abokya beiroshanō makabodara mani handoma jinbara harabaritaya un '''[[Korean language|Korean]]''' * 옴 아모가 바이로차나 마하무드라 마니 파드마 즈바라 프라바릍타야 훔 * Romanized: om amoga bairochana mahamudeura mani padeuma jeubara peurabareutaya hum '''[[Vietnamese language|Vietnamese]]''' * Án (Ông/Úm) a mộ già di lô tã nẵng, ma ha mẫu nại ra, na nĩ bát nạp mạ nhập phạ lã, nhập phạ lã, bác ra ni, mát đa dã hồng. '''[[Tibetan script|Tibetan]]''' * ཨོཾ་ཨ་མོ་གྷ་བཻ་རོ་ཙ་ན་མ་ཧཱ་མུ་དྲཱ་མ་ཎི་པདྨ​་ཛྭ་ལ་པྲ་ཝརྟཱ་ཡ་ཧཱུྃ === Translation === The translation of this mantra, according to Professor Mark Unno, is roughly: {{blockquote|Praise be to the flawless, all-pervasive illumination of the great [[mudra]] [or seal of the [[Buddha]] ]. Turn over to me the jewel, lotus and radiant light.}} Another translation according to the Dharmachakra Translation Committee is:
"Oṁ, amogha jewel-lotus of the splendorous great mudrā! Blaze! Set in motion! Hūṁ!"
== Use and history == [[File:Mandala of Mantra of Light.jpg|thumb|upright|right|Light Mantra Mandala]] The mantra appears in the ''Amoghapāśa-kalparāja-sūtra.'' It is used as a consecration mantra by the Buddhas of the ten directions which appear to ritually consecrate the bodhisattva Amoghapāśa. In the Tibetan version of this text, the passage which introduces the mantra is:
All the tathāgatas throughout the ten directions, the fully realized buddhas established throughout the three times, became clearly visible, appearing, through their magical power, to be suspended in the canopy of the sky. They applauded the blessed Tathāgata Śākyamuni: "Good! It is good, Śākyamuni! This king of consecrations is extremely difficult to obtain. The king of vidyās was also consecrated by the former tathāgatas established throughout the three times. All of us declare with a single voice that each of us will also consecrate the great king of vidyās, the Light of the Wish-Fulfilling Amogha Jewel." Subsequently, each of the tathāgatas established throughout the three times extended his golden hand and consecrated the king of vidyās by anointing his head. Each tathāgata thus bestowed the mudrā consecration upon the king of vidyās. The mantra for the mudrā consecration is: "Oṁ, amogha jewel-lotus of the splendorous great mudrā! Blaze! Set in motion! Hūṁ!" Immediately after bestowing the great mudrā of consecration, each tathāgata congratulated the king of vidyās and disappeared, returning to his particular buddha field.
Initially, the mantra received little mention in [[East Asian Buddhism|East Asian Buddhist]] texts. The mantra is found in a short text translated by [[Amoghavajra]] which focuses on its [[Apotropaic magic|apotropaic]] and healing uses (Taisho no. 1002, entitled ''Sutra of the Amoghapasa Light Mantra of the Buddha Vairocana’s Great Consecration'', 不空羂索毘盧遮那佛大灌頂光真言一卷). Although [[Kukai]] brought this sutra to Japan in the 9th century, there are no records that he ever utilized it in tantric practices. Records show gradually increasing use in the [[Heian Period]], until the 13th century when it was popularized in medieval [[Japanese Buddhism]] by [[Myoe|Myōe]], and later by [[Shingon]] monks [[Eison]] and [[Ninshō]] in their ministries.{{cite book | last = Tanabe Jr. | first = George | title = Religions of Japan in Practice | publisher = Princeton University Press | year = 1999 | isbn = 0-231-11286-6 | page=92}} Both the Mantra and the [[nembutsu]] were often incorporated by medieval Buddhists at one time or another, often in the same service. A common practice for the Mantra of Light was to sprinkle pure sand, blessed with this mantra, on the body of a deceased person or their tomb, based on teachings expounded in the Sutra. The belief was that a person who had accumulated much bad karma, and possible rebirth in [[Avici|Hell]] would be immediately freed and allowed a favorable rebirth into the [[Pure Land]] of Amitabha Buddha. This practice is known as {{nihongo|'''''dosha-kaji'''''|土砂加持}} in Japanese. Today, the mantra remains one of the most popular mantras in [[Shingon Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Buddhism topics}} [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:Buddhist mantras]] [[Category:Vairocana]]

{{Short description|Mantra in Japanese Buddhism}} {{Use dmy dates|date=August 2019}} The '''Mantra of Light''' ([[Japanese language|Japanese]]: ''kōmyō shingon'', 光明真言, Sanskrit: ''Prabhāsa-mantra''), also called the ''Mantra of the Light of Great Consecration'' (Ch: 大灌頂光真言) and ''Mantra of the Unfailing Rope Snare'', is an important [[mantra]] of the [[Shingon]] and [[Kegon]] sects of [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]]. It is also recited in Japanese [[Zen|Zen Buddhism]].Unno, Mark, ''Shingon Refractions: Myoe and the Mantra of Light,'' Ch. 1. The mantra is found in the ''Amoghapāśa-kalparāja-sūtra'' (Chinese translation at [[Taishō Tripiṭaka|Taisho]] no. 1092) as well as in the ''Sutra of the Mantra of the Unfailing Rope Snare of the Buddha Vairocana's Great Baptism'' (不空羂索毘盧遮那佛大灌頂光真言一卷, Taisho no. 1002) and is associated with the deity Amoghapāśa (lit. "Unfailing Rope"), a form of [[Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteshvara]]. It is also the mantra associated with the consecration (abhiseka) of Amoghapāśa by myriad Buddhas (hence its name as "mantra for the mudrā consecration" in the Tibetan version of the text)''.''{{cite book | last = Unno | first = Mark | title = Shingon Refractions: Myoe and the Mantra of Light | publisher = Wisdom Press | year = 2004 | isbn = 0-86171-390-7 | pages=1, 26–41}}{{Cite web |title=The Sovereign Ritual of Amoghapāśa {{!}} 84000 Reading Room |url=https://read.84000.co/translation/toh686.html?id=&part= |access-date=2022-12-06 |website=84000 Translating The Words of The Buddha |language=en}} == Mantra == [[file:Niutsuhimejinja 09.JPG|thumb|Stone stele of the Mantra of Light, Niutsuhime-jinja, Japan]] The "mantra of light" is the following: '''[[Sanskrit]]''' *Roman Script: Oṃ Amogha [[Vairocana]] [[Mahamudra|Mahāmudrā]] Maṇipadma Jvālapravarttaya Hūṃ *[[Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ]]: {{Script|Sidd|𑖌𑖼𑖀𑖦𑖺𑖑𑖪𑖹𑖨𑖺𑖓𑖡𑖦𑖮𑖯𑖦𑖲𑖟𑖿𑖨𑖯𑖦𑖜𑖰𑖢𑖟𑖿𑖦𑖕𑖿𑖪𑖯𑖩𑖢𑖿𑖨𑖪𑖨𑖿𑖝𑖿𑖝𑖧𑖮𑗝𑖽}} *[[Devanāgarī|Devanagari]]: ॐ अमोघ वैरोचन महामुद्रा मणिपद्म ज्वाल प्रवर्त्तय हूँ *{{Audio|Mantra_of_light.OGG|Sanskrit ''Pronunciation''}} '''[[Chinese language|Chinese script]]''' * 唵 阿謨伽 尾盧左曩 摩訶母捺囉 麽抳 鉢納麽 入嚩攞 鉢囉韈哆野 吽 * Ǎn ā mó jiā wěi lú zuǒ nǎng mó hē mǔ nà luō me nǐ bō nà me rù mó luó bō luō wà duō yě hōng '''[[Japanese language|Japanese]]''' * おん あぼきゃ べいろしゃのう まかぼだら まに はんどま じんばら はらばりたや うん * Romanized: Om abokya beiroshanō makabodara mani handoma jinbara harabaritaya un '''[[Korean language|Korean]]''' * 옴 아모가 바이로차나 마하무드라 마니 파드마 즈바라 프라바릍타야 훔 * Romanized: om amoga bairochana mahamudeura mani padeuma jeubara peurabareutaya hum '''[[Vietnamese language|Vietnamese]]''' * Án (Ông/Úm) a mộ già di lô tã nẵng, ma ha mẫu nại ra, na nĩ bát nạp mạ nhập phạ lã, nhập phạ lã, bác ra ni, mát đa dã hồng. '''[[Tibetan script|Tibetan]]''' * ཨོཾ་ཨ་མོ་གྷ་བཻ་རོ་ཙ་ན་མ་ཧཱ་མུ་དྲཱ་མ་ཎི་པདྨ​་ཛྭ་ལ་པྲ་ཝརྟཱ་ཡ་ཧཱུྃ === Translation === The translation of this mantra, according to Professor Mark Unno, is roughly: {{blockquote|Praise be to the flawless, all-pervasive illumination of the great [[mudra]] [or seal of the [[Buddha]] ]. Turn over to me the jewel, lotus and radiant light.}} Another translation according to the Dharmachakra Translation Committee is:
"Oṁ, amogha jewel-lotus of the splendorous great mudrā! Blaze! Set in motion! Hūṁ!"
== Use and history == [[File:Mandala of Mantra of Light.jpg|thumb|upright|right|Light Mantra Mandala]] The mantra appears in the ''Amoghapāśa-kalparāja-sūtra.'' It is used as a consecration mantra by the Buddhas of the ten directions which appear to ritually consecrate the bodhisattva Amoghapāśa. In the Tibetan version of this text, the passage which introduces the mantra is:
All the tathāgatas throughout the ten directions, the fully realized buddhas established throughout the three times, became clearly visible, appearing, through their magical power, to be suspended in the canopy of the sky. They applauded the blessed Tathāgata Śākyamuni: "Good! It is good, Śākyamuni! This king of consecrations is extremely difficult to obtain. The king of vidyās was also consecrated by the former tathāgatas established throughout the three times. All of us declare with a single voice that each of us will also consecrate the great king of vidyās, the Light of the Wish-Fulfilling Amogha Jewel." Subsequently, each of the tathāgatas established throughout the three times extended his golden hand and consecrated the king of vidyās by anointing his head. Each tathāgata thus bestowed the mudrā consecration upon the king of vidyās. The mantra for the mudrā consecration is: "Oṁ, amogha jewel-lotus of the splendorous great mudrā! Blaze! Set in motion! Hūṁ!" Immediately after bestowing the great mudrā of consecration, each tathāgata congratulated the king of vidyās and disappeared, returning to his particular buddha field.
Initially, the mantra received little mention in [[East Asian Buddhism|East Asian Buddhist]] texts. The mantra is found in a short text translated by [[Amoghavajra]] which focuses on its [[Apotropaic magic|apotropaic]] and healing uses (Taisho no. 1002, entitled ''Sutra of the Amoghapasa Light Mantra of the Buddha Vairocana’s Great Consecration'', 不空羂索毘盧遮那佛大灌頂光真言一卷). Although [[Kukai]] brought this sutra to Japan in the 9th century, there are no records that he ever utilized it in tantric practices. Records show gradually increasing use in the [[Heian Period]], until the 13th century when it was popularized in medieval [[Japanese Buddhism]] by [[Myoe|Myōe]], and later by [[Shingon]] monks [[Eison]] and [[Ninshō]] in their ministries.{{cite book | last = Tanabe Jr. | first = George | title = Religions of Japan in Practice | publisher = Princeton University Press | year = 1999 | isbn = 0-231-11286-6 | page=92}} Both the Mantra and the [[nembutsu]] were often incorporated by medieval Buddhists at one time or another, often in the same service. A common practice for the Mantra of Light was to sprinkle pure sand, blessed with this mantra, on the body of a deceased person or their tomb, based on teachings expounded in the Sutra. The belief was that a person who had accumulated much bad karma, and possible rebirth in [[Avici|Hell]] would be immediately freed and allowed a favorable rebirth into the [[Pure Land]] of Amitabha Buddha. This practice is known as {{nihongo|'''''dosha-kaji'''''|土砂加持}} in Japanese. Today, the mantra remains one of the most popular mantras in [[Shingon Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Buddhism topics}} [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:Buddhist mantras]] [[Category:Vairocana]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia0]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Mantra of Light]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]]

'''[[Tibetan script|Tibetan]]''' * ཨོཾ་ཨ་མོ་གྷ་བཻ་རོ་ཙ་ན་མ་ཧཱ་མུ་དྲཱ་མ་ཎི་པདྨ​་ཛྭ་ལ་པྲ་ཝརྟཱ་ཡ་ཧཱུྃ === Translation === The translation of this mantra, according to Professor Mark Unno, is roughly: {{blockquote|Praise be to the flawless, all-pervasive illumination of the great [[mudra]] [or seal of the [[Buddha]] ]. {{Short description|Mantra in Japanese Buddhism}} {{Use dmy dates|date=August 2019}} The '''Mantra of Light''' ([[Japanese language|Japanese]]: ''kōmyō shingon'', 光明真言, Sanskrit: ''Prabhāsa-mantra''), also called the ''Mantra of the Light of Great Consecration'' (Ch: 大灌頂光真言) and ''Mantra of the Unfailing Rope Snare'', is an important [[mantra]] of the [[Shingon]] and [[Kegon]] sects of [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]]. It is also recited in Japanese [[Zen|Zen Buddhism]].Unno, Mark, ''Shingon Refractions: Myoe and the Mantra of Light,'' Ch. 1. The mantra is found in the ''Amoghapāśa-kalparāja-sūtra'' (Chinese translation at [[Taishō Tripiṭaka|Taisho]] no. 1092) as well as in the ''Sutra of the Mantra of the Unfailing Rope Snare of the Buddha Vairocana's Great Baptism'' (不空羂索毘盧遮那佛大灌頂光真言一卷, Taisho no. 1002) and is associated with the deity Amoghapāśa (lit. "Unfailing Rope"), a form of [[Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteshvara]]. It is also the mantra associated with the consecration (abhiseka) of Amoghapāśa by myriad Buddhas (hence its name as "mantra for the mudrā consecration" in the Tibetan version of the text)''.''{{cite book | last = Unno | first = Mark | title = Shingon Refractions: Myoe and the Mantra of Light | publisher = Wisdom Press | year = 2004 | isbn = 0-86171-390-7 | pages=1, 26–41}}{{Cite web |title=The Sovereign Ritual of Amoghapāśa {{!}} 84000 Reading Room |url=https://read.84000.co/translation/toh686.html?id=&part= |access-date=2022-12-06 |website=84000 Translating The Words of The Buddha |language=en}} == Mantra == [[file:Niutsuhimejinja 09.JPG|thumb|Stone stele of the Mantra of Light, Niutsuhime-jinja, Japan]] The "mantra of light" is the following: '''[[Sanskrit]]''' *Roman Script: Oṃ Amogha [[Vairocana]] [[Mahamudra|Mahāmudrā]] Maṇipadma Jvālapravarttaya Hūṃ *[[Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ]]: {{Script|Sidd|𑖌𑖼𑖀𑖦𑖺𑖑𑖪𑖹𑖨𑖺𑖓𑖡𑖦𑖮𑖯𑖦𑖲𑖟𑖿𑖨𑖯𑖦𑖜𑖰𑖢𑖟𑖿𑖦𑖕𑖿𑖪𑖯𑖩𑖢𑖿𑖨𑖪𑖨𑖿𑖝𑖿𑖝𑖧𑖮𑗝𑖽}} *[[Devanāgarī|Devanagari]]: ॐ अमोघ वैरोचन महामुद्रा मणिपद्म ज्वाल प्रवर्त्तय हूँ *{{Audio|Mantra_of_light.OGG|Sanskrit ''Pronunciation''}} '''[[Chinese language|Chinese script]]''' * 唵 阿謨伽 尾盧左曩 摩訶母捺囉 麽抳 鉢納麽 入嚩攞 鉢囉韈哆野 吽 * Ǎn ā mó jiā wěi lú zuǒ nǎng mó hē mǔ nà luō me nǐ bō nà me rù mó luó bō luō wà duō yě hōng '''[[Japanese language|Japanese]]''' * おん あぼきゃ べいろしゃのう まかぼだら まに はんどま じんばら はらばりたや うん * Romanized: Om abokya beiroshanō makabodara mani handoma jinbara harabaritaya un '''[[Korean language|Korean]]''' * 옴 아모가 바이로차나 마하무드라 마니 파드마 즈바라 프라바릍타야 훔 * Romanized: om amoga bairochana mahamudeura mani padeuma jeubara peurabareutaya hum '''[[Vietnamese language|Vietnamese]]''' * Án (Ông/Úm) a mộ già di lô tã nẵng, ma ha mẫu nại ra, na nĩ bát nạp mạ nhập phạ lã, nhập phạ lã, bác ra ni, mát đa dã hồng. '''[[Tibetan script|Tibetan]]''' * ཨོཾ་ཨ་མོ་གྷ་བཻ་རོ་ཙ་ན་མ་ཧཱ་མུ་དྲཱ་མ་ཎི་པདྨ​་ཛྭ་ལ་པྲ་ཝརྟཱ་ཡ་ཧཱུྃ === Translation === The translation of this mantra, according to Professor Mark Unno, is roughly: {{blockquote|Praise be to the flawless, all-pervasive illumination of the great [[mudra]] [or seal of the [[Buddha]] ]. Turn over to me the jewel, lotus and radiant light.}} Another translation according to the Dharmachakra Translation Committee is:
"Oṁ, amogha jewel-lotus of the splendorous great mudrā! Blaze! Set in motion! Hūṁ!"
== Use and history == [[File:Mandala of Mantra of Light.jpg|thumb|upright|right|Light Mantra Mandala]] The mantra appears in the ''Amoghapāśa-kalparāja-sūtra.'' It is used as a consecration mantra by the Buddhas of the ten directions which appear to ritually consecrate the bodhisattva Amoghapāśa. In the Tibetan version of this text, the passage which introduces the mantra is:
All the tathāgatas throughout the ten directions, the fully realized buddhas established throughout the three times, became clearly visible, appearing, through their magical power, to be suspended in the canopy of the sky. They applauded the blessed Tathāgata Śākyamuni: "Good! It is good, Śākyamuni! This king of consecrations is extremely difficult to obtain. The king of vidyās was also consecrated by the former tathāgatas established throughout the three times. All of us declare with a single voice that each of us will also consecrate the great king of vidyās, the Light of the Wish-Fulfilling Amogha Jewel." Subsequently, each of the tathāgatas established throughout the three times extended his golden hand and consecrated the king of vidyās by anointing his head. Each tathāgata thus bestowed the mudrā consecration upon the king of vidyās. The mantra for the mudrā consecration is: "Oṁ, amogha jewel-lotus of the splendorous great mudrā! Blaze! Set in motion! Hūṁ!" Immediately after bestowing the great mudrā of consecration, each tathāgata congratulated the king of vidyās and disappeared, returning to his particular buddha field.
Initially, the mantra received little mention in [[East Asian Buddhism|East Asian Buddhist]] texts. The mantra is found in a short text translated by [[Amoghavajra]] which focuses on its [[Apotropaic magic|apotropaic]] and healing uses (Taisho no. 1002, entitled ''Sutra of the Amoghapasa Light Mantra of the Buddha Vairocana’s Great Consecration'', 不空羂索毘盧遮那佛大灌頂光真言一卷). Although [[Kukai]] brought this sutra to Japan in the 9th century, there are no records that he ever utilized it in tantric practices. Records show gradually increasing use in the [[Heian Period]], until the 13th century when it was popularized in medieval [[Japanese Buddhism]] by [[Myoe|Myōe]], and later by [[Shingon]] monks [[Eison]] and [[Ninshō]] in their ministries.{{cite book | last = Tanabe Jr. | first = George | title = Religions of Japan in Practice | publisher = Princeton University Press | year = 1999 | isbn = 0-231-11286-6 | page=92}} Both the Mantra and the [[nembutsu]] were often incorporated by medieval Buddhists at one time or another, often in the same service. A common practice for the Mantra of Light was to sprinkle pure sand, blessed with this mantra, on the body of a deceased person or their tomb, based on teachings expounded in the Sutra. The belief was that a person who had accumulated much bad karma, and possible rebirth in [[Avici|Hell]] would be immediately freed and allowed a favorable rebirth into the [[Pure Land]] of Amitabha Buddha. This practice is known as {{nihongo|'''''dosha-kaji'''''|土砂加持}} in Japanese. Today, the mantra remains one of the most popular mantras in [[Shingon Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Buddhism topics}} [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:Buddhist mantras]] [[Category:Vairocana]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia33]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Mantra of Light]]

{{Short description|Mantra in Japanese Buddhism}} {{Use dmy dates|date=August 2019}} The '''Mantra of Light''' ([[Japanese language|Japanese]]: ''kōmyō shingon'', 光明真言, Sanskrit: ''Prabhāsa-mantra''), also called the ''Mantra of the Light of Great Consecration'' (Ch: 大灌頂光真言) and ''Mantra of the Unfailing Rope Snare'', is an important [[mantra]] of the [[Shingon]] and [[Kegon]] sects of [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]]. It is also recited in Japanese [[Zen|Zen Buddhism]].Unno, Mark, ''Shingon Refractions: Myoe and the Mantra of Light,'' Ch. 1. The mantra is found in the ''Amoghapāśa-kalparāja-sūtra'' (Chinese translation at [[Taishō Tripiṭaka|Taisho]] no. 1092) as well as in the ''Sutra of the Mantra of the Unfailing Rope Snare of the Buddha Vairocana's Great Baptism'' (不空羂索毘盧遮那佛大灌頂光真言一卷, Taisho no. 1002) and is associated with the deity Amoghapāśa (lit. "Unfailing Rope"), a form of [[Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteshvara]]. It is also the mantra associated with the consecration (abhiseka) of Amoghapāśa by myriad Buddhas (hence its name as "mantra for the mudrā consecration" in the Tibetan version of the text)''.''{{cite book | last = Unno | first = Mark | title = Shingon Refractions: Myoe and the Mantra of Light | publisher = Wisdom Press | year = 2004 | isbn = 0-86171-390-7 | pages=1, 26–41}}{{Cite web |title=The Sovereign Ritual of Amoghapāśa {{!}} 84000 Reading Room |url=https://read.84000.co/translation/toh686.html?id=&part= |access-date=2022-12-06 |website=84000 Translating The Words of The Buddha |language=en}} == Mantra == [[file:Niutsuhimejinja 09.JPG|thumb|Stone stele of the Mantra of Light, Niutsuhime-jinja, Japan]] The "mantra of light" is the following: '''[[Sanskrit]]''' *Roman Script: Oṃ Amogha [[Vairocana]] [[Mahamudra|Mahāmudrā]] Maṇipadma Jvālapravarttaya Hūṃ *[[Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ]]: {{Script|Sidd|𑖌𑖼𑖀𑖦𑖺𑖑𑖪𑖹𑖨𑖺𑖓𑖡𑖦𑖮𑖯𑖦𑖲𑖟𑖿𑖨𑖯𑖦𑖜𑖰𑖢𑖟𑖿𑖦𑖕𑖿𑖪𑖯𑖩𑖢𑖿𑖨𑖪𑖨𑖿𑖝𑖿𑖝𑖧𑖮𑗝𑖽}} *[[Devanāgarī|Devanagari]]: ॐ अमोघ वैरोचन महामुद्रा मणिपद्म ज्वाल प्रवर्त्तय हूँ *{{Audio|Mantra_of_light.OGG|Sanskrit ''Pronunciation''}} '''[[Chinese language|Chinese script]]''' * 唵 阿謨伽 尾盧左曩 摩訶母捺囉 麽抳 鉢納麽 入嚩攞 鉢囉韈哆野 吽 * Ǎn ā mó jiā wěi lú zuǒ nǎng mó hē mǔ nà luō me nǐ bō nà me rù mó luó bō luō wà duō yě hōng '''[[Japanese language|Japanese]]''' * おん あぼきゃ べいろしゃのう まかぼだら まに はんどま じんばら はらばりたや うん * Romanized: Om abokya beiroshanō makabodara mani handoma jinbara harabaritaya un '''[[Korean language|Korean]]''' * 옴 아모가 바이로차나 마하무드라 마니 파드마 즈바라 프라바릍타야 훔 * Romanized: om amoga bairochana mahamudeura mani padeuma jeubara peurabareutaya hum '''[[Vietnamese language|Vietnamese]]''' * Án (Ông/Úm) a mộ già di lô tã nẵng, ma ha mẫu nại ra, na nĩ bát nạp mạ nhập phạ lã, nhập phạ lã, bác ra ni, mát đa dã hồng. '''[[Tibetan script|Tibetan]]''' * ཨོཾ་ཨ་མོ་གྷ་བཻ་རོ་ཙ་ན་མ་ཧཱ་མུ་དྲཱ་མ་ཎི་པདྨ​་ཛྭ་ལ་པྲ་ཝརྟཱ་ཡ་ཧཱུྃ === Translation === The translation of this mantra, according to Professor Mark Unno, is roughly: {{blockquote|Praise be to the flawless, all-pervasive illumination of the great [[mudra]] [or seal of the [[Buddha]] ]. Turn over to me the jewel, lotus and radiant light.}} Another translation according to the Dharmachakra Translation Committee is:
"Oṁ, amogha jewel-lotus of the splendorous great mudrā! Blaze! Set in motion! Hūṁ!"
== Use and history == [[File:Mandala of Mantra of Light.jpg|thumb|upright|right|Light Mantra Mandala]] The mantra appears in the ''Amoghapāśa-kalparāja-sūtra.'' It is used as a consecration mantra by the Buddhas of the ten directions which appear to ritually consecrate the bodhisattva Amoghapāśa. In the Tibetan version of this text, the passage which introduces the mantra is:
All the tathāgatas throughout the ten directions, the fully realized buddhas established throughout the three times, became clearly visible, appearing, through their magical power, to be suspended in the canopy of the sky. They applauded the blessed Tathāgata Śākyamuni: "Good! It is good, Śākyamuni! This king of consecrations is extremely difficult to obtain. The king of vidyās was also consecrated by the former tathāgatas established throughout the three times. All of us declare with a single voice that each of us will also consecrate the great king of vidyās, the Light of the Wish-Fulfilling Amogha Jewel." Subsequently, each of the tathāgatas established throughout the three times extended his golden hand and consecrated the king of vidyās by anointing his head. Each tathāgata thus bestowed the mudrā consecration upon the king of vidyās. The mantra for the mudrā consecration is: "Oṁ, amogha jewel-lotus of the splendorous great mudrā! Blaze! Set in motion! Hūṁ!" Immediately after bestowing the great mudrā of consecration, each tathāgata congratulated the king of vidyās and disappeared, returning to his particular buddha field.
Initially, the mantra received little mention in [[East Asian Buddhism|East Asian Buddhist]] texts. The mantra is found in a short text translated by [[Amoghavajra]] which focuses on its [[Apotropaic magic|apotropaic]] and healing uses (Taisho no. 1002, entitled ''Sutra of the Amoghapasa Light Mantra of the Buddha Vairocana’s Great Consecration'', 不空羂索毘盧遮那佛大灌頂光真言一卷). Although [[Kukai]] brought this sutra to Japan in the 9th century, there are no records that he ever utilized it in tantric practices. Records show gradually increasing use in the [[Heian Period]], until the 13th century when it was popularized in medieval [[Japanese Buddhism]] by [[Myoe|Myōe]], and later by [[Shingon]] monks [[Eison]] and [[Ninshō]] in their ministries.{{cite book | last = Tanabe Jr. | first = George | title = Religions of Japan in Practice | publisher = Princeton University Press | year = 1999 | isbn = 0-231-11286-6 | page=92}} Both the Mantra and the [[nembutsu]] were often incorporated by medieval Buddhists at one time or another, often in the same service. A common practice for the Mantra of Light was to sprinkle pure sand, blessed with this mantra, on the body of a deceased person or their tomb, based on teachings expounded in the Sutra. The belief was that a person who had accumulated much bad karma, and possible rebirth in [[Avici|Hell]] would be immediately freed and allowed a favorable rebirth into the [[Pure Land]] of Amitabha Buddha. This practice is known as {{nihongo|'''''dosha-kaji'''''|土砂加持}} in Japanese. Today, the mantra remains one of the most popular mantras in [[Shingon Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Buddhism topics}} [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:Buddhist mantras]] [[Category:Vairocana]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Mantra of Light]]

{{Short description|Mantra in Japanese Buddhism}} {{Use dmy dates|date=August 2019}} The '''Mantra of Light''' ([[Japanese language|Japanese]]: ''kōmyō shingon'', 光明真言, Sanskrit: ''Prabhāsa-mantra''), also called the ''Mantra of the Light of Great Consecration'' (Ch: 大灌頂光真言) and ''Mantra of the Unfailing Rope Snare'', is an important [[mantra]] of the [[Shingon]] and [[Kegon]] sects of [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]]. It is also recited in Japanese [[Zen|Zen Buddhism]].Unno, Mark, ''Shingon Refractions: Myoe and the Mantra of Light,'' Ch. 1. The mantra is found in the ''Amoghapāśa-kalparāja-sūtra'' (Chinese translation at [[Taishō Tripiṭaka|Taisho]] no. 1092) as well as in the ''Sutra of the Mantra of the Unfailing Rope Snare of the Buddha Vairocana's Great Baptism'' (不空羂索毘盧遮那佛大灌頂光真言一卷, Taisho no. 1002) and is associated with the deity Amoghapāśa (lit. "Unfailing Rope"), a form of [[Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteshvara]]. It is also the mantra associated with the consecration (abhiseka) of Amoghapāśa by myriad Buddhas (hence its name as "mantra for the mudrā consecration" in the Tibetan version of the text)''.''{{cite book | last = Unno | first = Mark | title = Shingon Refractions: Myoe and the Mantra of Light | publisher = Wisdom Press | year = 2004 | isbn = 0-86171-390-7 | pages=1, 26–41}}{{Cite web |title=The Sovereign Ritual of Amoghapāśa {{!}} 84000 Reading Room |url=https://read.84000.co/translation/toh686.html?id=&part= |access-date=2022-12-06 |website=84000 Translating The Words of The Buddha |language=en}} == Mantra == [[file:Niutsuhimejinja 09.JPG|thumb|Stone stele of the Mantra of Light, Niutsuhime-jinja, Japan]] The "mantra of light" is the following: '''[[Sanskrit]]''' *Roman Script: Oṃ Amogha [[Vairocana]] [[Mahamudra|Mahāmudrā]] Maṇipadma Jvālapravarttaya Hūṃ *[[Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ]]: {{Script|Sidd|𑖌𑖼𑖀𑖦𑖺𑖑𑖪𑖹𑖨𑖺𑖓𑖡𑖦𑖮𑖯𑖦𑖲𑖟𑖿𑖨𑖯𑖦𑖜𑖰𑖢𑖟𑖿𑖦𑖕𑖿𑖪𑖯𑖩𑖢𑖿𑖨𑖪𑖨𑖿𑖝𑖿𑖝𑖧𑖮𑗝𑖽}} *[[Devanāgarī|Devanagari]]: ॐ अमोघ वैरोचन महामुद्रा मणिपद्म ज्वाल प्रवर्त्तय हूँ *{{Audio|Mantra_of_light.OGG|Sanskrit ''Pronunciation''}} '''[[Chinese language|Chinese script]]''' * 唵 阿謨伽 尾盧左曩 摩訶母捺囉 麽抳 鉢納麽 入嚩攞 鉢囉韈哆野 吽 * Ǎn ā mó jiā wěi lú zuǒ nǎng mó hē mǔ nà luō me nǐ bō nà me rù mó luó bō luō wà duō yě hōng '''[[Japanese language|Japanese]]''' * おん あぼきゃ べいろしゃのう まかぼだら まに はんどま じんばら はらばりたや うん * Romanized: Om abokya beiroshanō makabodara mani handoma jinbara harabaritaya un '''[[Korean language|Korean]]''' * 옴 아모가 바이로차나 마하무드라 마니 파드마 즈바라 프라바릍타야 훔 * Romanized: om amoga bairochana mahamudeura mani padeuma jeubara peurabareutaya hum '''[[Vietnamese language|Vietnamese]]''' * Án (Ông/Úm) a mộ già di lô tã nẵng, ma ha mẫu nại ra, na nĩ bát nạp mạ nhập phạ lã, nhập phạ lã, bác ra ni, mát đa dã hồng. '''[[Tibetan script|Tibetan]]''' * ཨོཾ་ཨ་མོ་གྷ་བཻ་རོ་ཙ་ན་མ་ཧཱ་མུ་དྲཱ་མ་ཎི་པདྨ​་ཛྭ་ལ་པྲ་ཝརྟཱ་ཡ་ཧཱུྃ === Translation === The translation of this mantra, according to Professor Mark Unno, is roughly: {{blockquote|Praise be to the flawless, all-pervasive illumination of the great [[mudra]] [or seal of the [[Buddha]] ]. Turn over to me the jewel, lotus and radiant light.}} Another translation according to the Dharmachakra Translation Committee is:
"Oṁ, amogha jewel-lotus of the splendorous great mudrā! Blaze! Set in motion! Hūṁ!"
== Use and history == [[File:Mandala of Mantra of Light.jpg|thumb|upright|right|Light Mantra Mandala]] The mantra appears in the ''Amoghapāśa-kalparāja-sūtra.'' It is used as a consecration mantra by the Buddhas of the ten directions which appear to ritually consecrate the bodhisattva Amoghapāśa. In the Tibetan version of this text, the passage which introduces the mantra is:
All the tathāgatas throughout the ten directions, the fully realized buddhas established throughout the three times, became clearly visible, appearing, through their magical power, to be suspended in the canopy of the sky. They applauded the blessed Tathāgata Śākyamuni: "Good! It is good, Śākyamuni! This king of consecrations is extremely difficult to obtain. The king of vidyās was also consecrated by the former tathāgatas established throughout the three times. All of us declare with a single voice that each of us will also consecrate the great king of vidyās, the Light of the Wish-Fulfilling Amogha Jewel." Subsequently, each of the tathāgatas established throughout the three times extended his golden hand and consecrated the king of vidyās by anointing his head. Each tathāgata thus bestowed the mudrā consecration upon the king of vidyās. The mantra for the mudrā consecration is: "Oṁ, amogha jewel-lotus of the splendorous great mudrā! Blaze! Set in motion! Hūṁ!" Immediately after bestowing the great mudrā of consecration, each tathāgata congratulated the king of vidyās and disappeared, returning to his particular buddha field.
Initially, the mantra received little mention in [[East Asian Buddhism|East Asian Buddhist]] texts. The mantra is found in a short text translated by [[Amoghavajra]] which focuses on its [[Apotropaic magic|apotropaic]] and healing uses (Taisho no. 1002, entitled ''Sutra of the Amoghapasa Light Mantra of the Buddha Vairocana’s Great Consecration'', 不空羂索毘盧遮那佛大灌頂光真言一卷). Although [[Kukai]] brought this sutra to Japan in the 9th century, there are no records that he ever utilized it in tantric practices. Records show gradually increasing use in the [[Heian Period]], until the 13th century when it was popularized in medieval [[Japanese Buddhism]] by [[Myoe|Myōe]], and later by [[Shingon]] monks [[Eison]] and [[Ninshō]] in their ministries.{{cite book | last = Tanabe Jr. | first = George | title = Religions of Japan in Practice | publisher = Princeton University Press | year = 1999 | isbn = 0-231-11286-6 | page=92}} Both the Mantra and the [[nembutsu]] were often incorporated by medieval Buddhists at one time or another, often in the same service. A common practice for the Mantra of Light was to sprinkle pure sand, blessed with this mantra, on the body of a deceased person or their tomb, based on teachings expounded in the Sutra. The belief was that a person who had accumulated much bad karma, and possible rebirth in [[Avici|Hell]] would be immediately freed and allowed a favorable rebirth into the [[Pure Land]] of Amitabha Buddha. This practice is known as {{nihongo|'''''dosha-kaji'''''|土砂加持}} in Japanese. Today, the mantra remains one of the most popular mantras in [[Shingon Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Buddhism topics}} [[category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[category:Buddhist mantras]] [[category:Vairocana]] [[category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[en:Mantra of Light]]

{{Short description|Mantra in Japanese Buddhism}} {{Use dmy dates|date=August 2019}} The '''Mantra of Light''' ([[Japanese language|Japanese]]: ''kōmyō shingon'', 光明真言, Sanskrit: ''Prabhāsa-mantra''), also called the ''Mantra of the Light of Great Consecration'' (Ch: 大灌頂光真言) and ''Mantra of the Unfailing Rope Snare'', is an important [[mantra]] of the [[Shingon]] and [[Kegon]] sects of [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]]. It is also recited in Japanese [[Zen|Zen Buddhism]].Unno, Mark, ''Shingon Refractions: Myoe and the Mantra of Light,'' Ch. 1. The mantra is found in the ''Amoghapāśa-kalparāja-sūtra'' (Chinese translation at [[Taishō Tripiṭaka|Taisho]] no. 1092) as well as in the ''Sutra of the Mantra of the Unfailing Rope Snare of the Buddha Vairocana's Great Baptism'' (不空羂索毘盧遮那佛大灌頂光真言一卷, Taisho no. 1002) and is associated with the deity Amoghapāśa (lit. "Unfailing Rope"), a form of [[Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteshvara]]. It is also the mantra associated with the consecration (abhiseka) of Amoghapāśa by myriad Buddhas (hence its name as "mantra for the mudrā consecration" in the Tibetan version of the text)''.''{{cite book | last = Unno | first = Mark | title = Shingon Refractions: Myoe and the Mantra of Light | publisher = Wisdom Press | year = 2004 | isbn = 0-86171-390-7 | pages=1, 26–41}}{{Cite web |title=The Sovereign Ritual of Amoghapāśa {{!}} 84000 Reading Room |url=https://read.84000.co/translation/toh686.html?id=&part= |access-date=2022-12-06 |website=84000 Translating The Words of The Buddha |language=en}} == Mantra == [[file:Niutsuhimejinja 09.JPG|thumb|Stone stele of the Mantra of Light, Niutsuhime-jinja, Japan]] The "mantra of light" is the following: '''[[Sanskrit]]''' *Roman Script: Oṃ Amogha [[Vairocana]] [[Mahamudra|Mahāmudrā]] Maṇipadma Jvālapravarttaya Hūṃ *[[Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ]]: {{Script|Sidd|𑖌𑖼𑖀𑖦𑖺𑖑𑖪𑖹𑖨𑖺𑖓𑖡𑖦𑖮𑖯𑖦𑖲𑖟𑖿𑖨𑖯𑖦𑖜𑖰𑖢𑖟𑖿𑖦𑖕𑖿𑖪𑖯𑖩𑖢𑖿𑖨𑖪𑖨𑖿𑖝𑖿𑖝𑖧𑖮𑗝𑖽}} *[[Devanāgarī|Devanagari]]: ॐ अमोघ वैरोचन महामुद्रा मणिपद्म ज्वाल प्रवर्त्तय हूँ *{{Audio|Mantra_of_light.OGG|Sanskrit ''Pronunciation''}} '''[[Chinese language|Chinese script]]''' * 唵 阿謨伽 尾盧左曩 摩訶母捺囉 麽抳 鉢納麽 入嚩攞 鉢囉韈哆野 吽 * Ǎn ā mó jiā wěi lú zuǒ nǎng mó hē mǔ nà luō me nǐ bō nà me rù mó luó bō luō wà duō yě hōng '''[[Japanese language|Japanese]]''' * おん あぼきゃ べいろしゃのう まかぼだら まに はんどま じんばら はらばりたや うん * Romanized: Om abokya beiroshanō makabodara mani handoma jinbara harabaritaya un '''[[Korean language|Korean]]''' * 옴 아모가 바이로차나 마하무드라 마니 파드마 즈바라 프라바릍타야 훔 * Romanized: om amoga bairochana mahamudeura mani padeuma jeubara peurabareutaya hum '''[[Vietnamese language|Vietnamese]]''' * Án (Ông/Úm) a mộ già di lô tã nẵng, ma ha mẫu nại ra, na nĩ bát nạp mạ nhập phạ lã, nhập phạ lã, bác ra ni, mát đa dã hồng. '''[[Tibetan script|Tibetan]]''' * ཨོཾ་ཨ་མོ་གྷ་བཻ་རོ་ཙ་ན་མ་ཧཱ་མུ་དྲཱ་མ་ཎི་པདྨ​་ཛྭ་ལ་པྲ་ཝརྟཱ་ཡ་ཧཱུྃ === Translation === The translation of this mantra, according to Professor Mark Unno, is roughly: {{blockquote|Praise be to the flawless, all-pervasive illumination of the great [[mudra]] [or seal of the [[Buddha]] ]. Turn over to me the jewel, lotus and radiant light.}} Another translation according to the Dharmachakra Translation Committee is:
"Oṁ, amogha jewel-lotus of the splendorous great mudrā! Blaze! Set in motion! Hūṁ!"

The king of vidyās was also consecrated by the former tathāgatas established throughout the three times. All of us declare with a single voice that each of us will also consecrate the great king of vidyās, the Light of the Wish-Fulfilling Amogha Jewel." Subsequently, each of the tathāgatas established throughout the three times extended his golden hand and consecrated the king of vidyās by anointing his head. This practice is known as {{nihongo|'''''dosha-kaji'''''|土砂加持}} in Japanese. Today, the mantra remains one of the most popular mantras in [[Shingon Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Buddhism topics}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Buddhist mantras]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Vairocana]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[en:Mantra of Light]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Mantra in Japanese Buddhism}} {{Use dmy dates|date=August 2019}} The '''Mantra of Light''' ([[Japanese language|Japanese]]: ''kōmyō shingon'', 光明真言, Sanskrit: ''Prabhāsa-mantra''), also called the ''Mantra of the Light of Great Consecration'' (Ch: 大灌頂光真言) and ''Mantra of the Unfailing Rope Snare'', is an important [[mantra]] of the [[Shingon]] and [[Kegon]] sects of [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]]. It is also recited in Japanese [[Zen|Zen Buddhism]].Unno, Mark, ''Shingon Refractions: Myoe and the Mantra of Light,'' Ch. 1. The mantra is found in the ''Amoghapāśa-kalparāja-sūtra'' (Chinese translation at [[Taishō Tripiṭaka|Taisho]] no. 1092) as well as in the ''Sutra of the Mantra of the Unfailing Rope Snare of the Buddha Vairocana's Great Baptism'' (不空羂索毘盧遮那佛大灌頂光真言一卷, Taisho no. 1002) and is associated with the deity Amoghapāśa (lit. "Unfailing Rope"), a form of [[Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteshvara]]. It is also the mantra associated with the consecration (abhiseka) of Amoghapāśa by myriad Buddhas (hence its name as "mantra for the mudrā consecration" in the Tibetan version of the text)''.''{{cite book | last = Unno | first = Mark | title = Shingon Refractions: Myoe and the Mantra of Light | publisher = Wisdom Press | year = 2004 | isbn = 0-86171-390-7 | pages=1, 26–41}}{{Cite web |title=The Sovereign Ritual of Amoghapāśa {{!}} 84000 Reading Room |url=https://read.84000.co/translation/toh686.html?id=&part= |access-date=2022-12-06 |website=84000 Translating The Words of The Buddha |language=en}} == Mantra == [[file:Niutsuhimejinja 09.JPG|thumb|Stone stele of the Mantra of Light, Niutsuhime-jinja, Japan]] The "mantra of light" is the following: '''[[Sanskrit]]''' *Roman Script: Oṃ Amogha [[Vairocana]] [[Mahamudra|Mahāmudrā]] Maṇipadma Jvālapravarttaya Hūṃ *[[Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ]]: {{Script|Sidd|𑖌𑖼𑖀𑖦𑖺𑖑𑖪𑖹𑖨𑖺𑖓𑖡𑖦𑖮𑖯𑖦𑖲𑖟𑖿𑖨𑖯𑖦𑖜𑖰𑖢𑖟𑖿𑖦𑖕𑖿𑖪𑖯𑖩𑖢𑖿𑖨𑖪𑖨𑖿𑖝𑖿𑖝𑖧𑖮𑗝𑖽}} *[[Devanāgarī|Devanagari]]: ॐ अमोघ वैरोचन महामुद्रा मणिपद्म ज्वाल प्रवर्त्तय हूँ *{{Audio|Mantra_of_light.OGG|Sanskrit ''Pronunciation''}} '''[[Chinese language|Chinese script]]''' * 唵 阿謨伽 尾盧左曩 摩訶母捺囉 麽抳 鉢納麽 入嚩攞 鉢囉韈哆野 吽 * Ǎn ā mó jiā wěi lú zuǒ nǎng mó hē mǔ nà luō me nǐ bō nà me rù mó luó bō luō wà duō yě hōng '''[[Japanese language|Japanese]]''' * おん あぼきゃ べいろしゃのう まかぼだら まに はんどま じんばら はらばりたや うん * Romanized: Om abokya beiroshanō makabodara mani handoma jinbara harabaritaya un '''[[Korean language|Korean]]''' * 옴 아모가 바이로차나 마하무드라 마니 파드마 즈바라 프라바릍타야 훔 * Romanized: om amoga bairochana mahamudeura mani padeuma jeubara peurabareutaya hum '''[[Vietnamese language|Vietnamese]]''' * Án (Ông/Úm) a mộ già di lô tã nẵng, ma ha mẫu nại ra, na nĩ bát nạp mạ nhập phạ lã, nhập phạ lã, bác ra ni, mát đa dã hồng. '''[[Tibetan script|Tibetan]]''' * ཨོཾ་ཨ་མོ་གྷ་བཻ་རོ་ཙ་ན་མ་ཧཱ་མུ་དྲཱ་མ་ཎི་པདྨ​་ཛྭ་ལ་པྲ་ཝརྟཱ་ཡ་ཧཱུྃ === Translation === The translation of this mantra, according to Professor Mark Unno, is roughly: {{blockquote|Praise be to the flawless, all-pervasive illumination of the great [[mudra]] [or seal of the [[Buddha]] ]. Turn over to me the jewel, lotus and radiant light.}} Another translation according to the Dharmachakra Translation Committee is:
"Oṁ, amogha jewel-lotus of the splendorous great mudrā! Blaze! Set in motion! Hūṁ!"
== Use and history == [[File:Mandala of Mantra of Light.jpg|thumb|upright|right|Light Mantra Mandala]] The mantra appears in the ''Amoghapāśa-kalparāja-sūtra.'' It is used as a consecration mantra by the Buddhas of the ten directions which appear to ritually consecrate the bodhisattva Amoghapāśa. In the Tibetan version of this text, the passage which introduces the mantra is:
All the tathāgatas throughout the ten directions, the fully realized buddhas established throughout the three times, became clearly visible, appearing, through their magical power, to be suspended in the canopy of the sky. They applauded the blessed Tathāgata Śākyamuni: "Good! It is good, Śākyamuni! This king of consecrations is extremely difficult to obtain. The king of vidyās was also consecrated by the former tathāgatas established throughout the three times. All of us declare with a single voice that each of us will also consecrate the great king of vidyās, the Light of the Wish-Fulfilling Amogha Jewel." Subsequently, each of the tathāgatas established throughout the three times extended his golden hand and consecrated the king of vidyās by anointing his head. Each tathāgata thus bestowed the mudrā consecration upon the king of vidyās. The mantra for the mudrā consecration is: "Oṁ, amogha jewel-lotus of the splendorous great mudrā! Blaze! Set in motion! Hūṁ!" Immediately after bestowing the great mudrā of consecration, each tathāgata congratulated the king of vidyās and disappeared, returning to his particular buddha field.
Initially, the mantra received little mention in [[East Asian Buddhism|East Asian Buddhist]] texts. The mantra is found in a short text translated by [[Amoghavajra]] which focuses on its [[Apotropaic magic|apotropaic]] and healing uses (Taisho no. 1002, entitled ''Sutra of the Amoghapasa Light Mantra of the Buddha Vairocana’s Great Consecration'', 不空羂索毘盧遮那佛大灌頂光真言一卷). Although [[Kukai]] brought this sutra to Japan in the 9th century, there are no records that he ever utilized it in tantric practices. Records show gradually increasing use in the [[Heian Period]], until the 13th century when it was popularized in medieval [[Japanese Buddhism]] by [[Myoe|Myōe]], and later by [[Shingon]] monks [[Eison]] and [[Ninshō]] in their ministries.{{cite book | last = Tanabe Jr. | first = George | title = Religions of Japan in Practice | publisher = Princeton University Press | year = 1999 | isbn = 0-231-11286-6 | page=92}} Both the Mantra and the [[nembutsu]] were often incorporated by medieval Buddhists at one time or another, often in the same service. A common practice for the Mantra of Light was to sprinkle pure sand, blessed with this mantra, on the body of a deceased person or their tomb, based on teachings expounded in the Sutra. The belief was that a person who had accumulated much bad karma, and possible rebirth in [[Avici|Hell]] would be immediately freed and allowed a favorable rebirth into the [[Pure Land]] of Amitabha Buddha. This practice is known as {{nihongo|'''''dosha-kaji'''''|土砂加持}} in Japanese. Today, the mantra remains one of the most popular mantras in [[Shingon Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Buddhism topics}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Buddhist mantras]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Vairocana]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Mantra of Light]]

They applauded the blessed Tathāgata Śākyamuni: "Good! It is good, Śākyamuni! This king of consecrations is extremely difficult to obtain. The king of vidyās was also consecrated by the former tathāgatas established throughout the three times. All of us declare with a single voice that each of us will also consecrate the great king of vidyās, the Light of the Wish-Fulfilling Amogha Jewel." Subsequently, each of the tathāgatas established throughout the three times extended his golden hand and consecrated the king of vidyās by anointing his head. Each tathāgata thus bestowed the mudrā consecration upon the king of vidyās. The mantra for the mudrā consecration is: "Oṁ, amogha jewel-lotus of the splendorous great mudrā! Blaze! Set in motion! Hūṁ!" Immediately after bestowing the great mudrā of consecration, each tathāgata congratulated the king of vidyās and disappeared, returning to his particular buddha field.
Initially, the mantra received little mention in [[East Asian Buddhism|East Asian Buddhist]] texts. The mantra is found in a short text translated by [[Amoghavajra]] which focuses on its [[Apotropaic magic|apotropaic]] and healing uses (Taisho no. 1002, entitled ''Sutra of the Amoghapasa Light Mantra of the Buddha Vairocana’s Great Consecration'', 不空羂索毘盧遮那佛大灌頂光真言一卷). Although [[Kukai]] brought this sutra to Japan in the 9th century, there are no records that he ever utilized it in tantric practices. Records show gradually increasing use in the [[Heian Period]], until the 13th century when it was popularized in medieval [[Japanese Buddhism]] by [[Myoe|Myōe]], and later by [[Shingon]] monks [[Eison]] and [[Ninshō]] in their ministries.{{cite book | last = Tanabe Jr. | first = George | title = Religions of Japan in Practice | publisher = Princeton University Press | year = 1999 | isbn = 0-231-11286-6 | page=92}} Both the Mantra and the [[nembutsu]] were often incorporated by medieval Buddhists at one time or another, often in the same service. A common practice for the Mantra of Light was to sprinkle pure sand, blessed with this mantra, on the body of a deceased person or their tomb, based on teachings expounded in the Sutra. The belief was that a person who had accumulated much bad karma, and possible rebirth in [[Avici|Hell]] would be immediately freed and allowed a favorable rebirth into the [[Pure Land]] of Amitabha Buddha. This practice is known as {{nihongo|'''''dosha-kaji'''''|土砂加持}} in Japanese. Today, the mantra remains one of the most popular mantras in [[Shingon Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Buddhism topics}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Buddhist mantras]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Vairocana]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mantra of Light]]

{{Short description|Mantra in Japanese Buddhism}} {{Use dmy dates|date=August 2019}} The '''Mantra of Light''' ([[Japanese language|Japanese]]: ''kōmyō shingon'', 光明真言, Sanskrit: ''Prabhāsa-mantra''), also called the ''Mantra of the Light of Great Consecration'' (Ch: 大灌頂光真言) and ''Mantra of the Unfailing Rope Snare'', is an important [[mantra]] of the [[Shingon]] and [[Kegon]] sects of [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]]. It is also recited in Japanese [[Zen|Zen Buddhism]].Unno, Mark, ''Shingon Refractions: Myoe and the Mantra of Light,'' Ch. 1. The mantra is found in the ''Amoghapāśa-kalparāja-sūtra'' (Chinese translation at [[Taishō Tripiṭaka|Taisho]] no. 1092) as well as in the ''Sutra of the Mantra of the Unfailing Rope Snare of the Buddha Vairocana's Great Baptism'' (不空羂索毘盧遮那佛大灌頂光真言一卷, Taisho no. 1002) and is associated with the deity Amoghapāśa (lit. "Unfailing Rope"), a form of [[Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteshvara]]. It is also the mantra associated with the consecration (abhiseka) of Amoghapāśa by myriad Buddhas (hence its name as "mantra for the mudrā consecration" in the Tibetan version of the text)''.''{{cite book | last = Unno | first = Mark | title = Shingon Refractions: Myoe and the Mantra of Light | publisher = Wisdom Press | year = 2004 | isbn = 0-86171-390-7 | pages=1, 26–41}}{{Cite web |title=The Sovereign Ritual of Amoghapāśa {{!}} 84000 Reading Room |url=https://read.84000.co/translation/toh686.html?id=&part= |access-date=2022-12-06 |website=84000 Translating The Words of The Buddha |language=en}} == Mantra == [[file:Niutsuhimejinja 09.JPG|thumb|Stone stele of the Mantra of Light, Niutsuhime-jinja, Japan]] The "mantra of light" is the following: '''[[Sanskrit]]''' *Roman Script: Oṃ Amogha [[Vairocana]] [[Mahamudra|Mahāmudrā]] Maṇipadma Jvālapravarttaya Hūṃ *[[Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ]]: {{Script|Sidd|𑖌𑖼𑖀𑖦𑖺𑖑𑖪𑖹𑖨𑖺𑖓𑖡𑖦𑖮𑖯𑖦𑖲𑖟𑖿𑖨𑖯𑖦𑖜𑖰𑖢𑖟𑖿𑖦𑖕𑖿𑖪𑖯𑖩𑖢𑖿𑖨𑖪𑖨𑖿𑖝𑖿𑖝𑖧𑖮𑗝𑖽}} *[[Devanāgarī|Devanagari]]: ॐ अमोघ वैरोचन महामुद्रा मणिपद्म ज्वाल प्रवर्त्तय हूँ *{{Audio|Mantra_of_light.OGG|Sanskrit ''Pronunciation''}} '''[[Chinese language|Chinese script]]''' * 唵 阿謨伽 尾盧左曩 摩訶母捺囉 麽抳 鉢納麽 入嚩攞 鉢囉韈哆野 吽 * Ǎn ā mó jiā wěi lú zuǒ nǎng mó hē mǔ nà luō me nǐ bō nà me rù mó luó bō luō wà duō yě hōng '''[[Japanese language|Japanese]]''' * おん あぼきゃ べいろしゃのう まかぼだら まに はんどま じんばら はらばりたや うん * Romanized: Om abokya beiroshanō makabodara mani handoma jinbara harabaritaya un '''[[Korean language|Korean]]''' * 옴 아모가 바이로차나 마하무드라 마니 파드마 즈바라 프라바릍타야 훔 * Romanized: om amoga bairochana mahamudeura mani padeuma jeubara peurabareutaya hum '''[[Vietnamese language|Vietnamese]]''' * Án (Ông/Úm) a mộ già di lô tã nẵng, ma ha mẫu nại ra, na nĩ bát nạp mạ nhập phạ lã, nhập phạ lã, bác ra ni, mát đa dã hồng. '''[[Tibetan script|Tibetan]]''' * ཨོཾ་ཨ་མོ་གྷ་བཻ་རོ་ཙ་ན་མ་ཧཱ་མུ་དྲཱ་མ་ཎི་པདྨ​་ཛྭ་ལ་པྲ་ཝརྟཱ་ཡ་ཧཱུྃ === Translation === The translation of this mantra, according to Professor Mark Unno, is roughly: {{blockquote|Praise be to the flawless, all-pervasive illumination of the great [[mudra]] [or seal of the [[Buddha]] ]. Turn over to me the jewel, lotus and radiant light.}} Another translation according to the Dharmachakra Translation Committee is:
"Oṁ, amogha jewel-lotus of the splendorous great mudrā! Blaze! Set in motion! Hūṁ!"
== Use and history == [[File:Mandala of Mantra of Light.jpg|thumb|upright|right|Light Mantra Mandala]] The mantra appears in the ''Amoghapāśa-kalparāja-sūtra.'' It is used as a consecration mantra by the Buddhas of the ten directions which appear to ritually consecrate the bodhisattva Amoghapāśa. In the Tibetan version of this text, the passage which introduces the mantra is:
All the tathāgatas throughout the ten directions, the fully realized buddhas established throughout the three times, became clearly visible, appearing, through their magical power, to be suspended in the canopy of the sky. They applauded the blessed Tathāgata Śākyamuni: "Good! It is good, Śākyamuni! This king of consecrations is extremely difficult to obtain. The king of vidyās was also consecrated by the former tathāgatas established throughout the three times. All of us declare with a single voice that each of us will also consecrate the great king of vidyās, the Light of the Wish-Fulfilling Amogha Jewel." Subsequently, each of the tathāgatas established throughout the three times extended his golden hand and consecrated the king of vidyās by anointing his head. Each tathāgata thus bestowed the mudrā consecration upon the king of vidyās. The mantra for the mudrā consecration is: "Oṁ, amogha jewel-lotus of the splendorous great mudrā! Blaze! Set in motion! Hūṁ!" Immediately after bestowing the great mudrā of consecration, each tathāgata congratulated the king of vidyās and disappeared, returning to his particular buddha field.
Initially, the mantra received little mention in [[East Asian Buddhism|East Asian Buddhist]] texts. The mantra is found in a short text translated by [[Amoghavajra]] which focuses on its [[Apotropaic magic|apotropaic]] and healing uses (Taisho no. 1002, entitled ''Sutra of the Amoghapasa Light Mantra of the Buddha Vairocana’s Great Consecration'', 不空羂索毘盧遮那佛大灌頂光真言一卷). Although [[Kukai]] brought this sutra to Japan in the 9th century, there are no records that he ever utilized it in tantric practices. Records show gradually increasing use in the [[Heian Period]], until the 13th century when it was popularized in medieval [[Japanese Buddhism]] by [[Myoe|Myōe]], and later by [[Shingon]] monks [[Eison]] and [[Ninshō]] in their ministries.{{cite book | last = Tanabe Jr. | first = George | title = Religions of Japan in Practice | publisher = Princeton University Press | year = 1999 | isbn = 0-231-11286-6 | page=92}} Both the Mantra and the [[nembutsu]] were often incorporated by medieval Buddhists at one time or another, often in the same service. A common practice for the Mantra of Light was to sprinkle pure sand, blessed with this mantra, on the body of a deceased person or their tomb, based on teachings expounded in the Sutra. The belief was that a person who had accumulated much bad karma, and possible rebirth in [[Avici|Hell]] would be immediately freed and allowed a favorable rebirth into the [[Pure Land]] of Amitabha Buddha. This practice is known as {{nihongo|'''''dosha-kaji'''''|土砂加持}} in Japanese. Today, the mantra remains one of the most popular mantras in [[Shingon Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Buddhism topics}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Buddhist mantras]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Vairocana]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:dardar]] [[en:Mantra of Light]]

{{Short description|Mantra in Japanese Buddhism}} {{Use dmy dates|date=August 2019}} The '''Mantra of Light''' ([[Japanese language|Japanese]]: ''kōmyō shingon'', 光明真言, Sanskrit: ''Prabhāsa-mantra''), also called the ''Mantra of the Light of Great Consecration'' (Ch: 大灌頂光真言) and ''Mantra of the Unfailing Rope Snare'', is an important [[mantra]] of the [[Shingon]] and [[Kegon]] sects of [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]]. It is also recited in Japanese [[Zen|Zen Buddhism]].Unno, Mark, ''Shingon Refractions: Myoe and the Mantra of Light,'' Ch. 1. The mantra is found in the ''Amoghapāśa-kalparāja-sūtra'' (Chinese translation at [[Taishō Tripiṭaka|Taisho]] no. 1092) as well as in the ''Sutra of the Mantra of the Unfailing Rope Snare of the Buddha Vairocana's Great Baptism'' (不空羂索毘盧遮那佛大灌頂光真言一卷, Taisho no. 1002) and is associated with the deity Amoghapāśa (lit. "Unfailing Rope"), a form of [[Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteshvara]]. It is also the mantra associated with the consecration (abhiseka) of Amoghapāśa by myriad Buddhas (hence its name as "mantra for the mudrā consecration" in the Tibetan version of the text)''.''{{cite book | last = Unno | first = Mark | title = Shingon Refractions: Myoe and the Mantra of Light | publisher = Wisdom Press | year = 2004 | isbn = 0-86171-390-7 | pages=1, 26–41}}{{Cite web |title=The Sovereign Ritual of Amoghapāśa {{!}} 84000 Reading Room |url=https://read.84000.co/translation/toh686.html?id=&part= |access-date=2022-12-06 |website=84000 Translating The Words of The Buddha |language=en}} == Mantra == [[file:Niutsuhimejinja 09.JPG|thumb|Stone stele of the Mantra of Light, Niutsuhime-jinja, Japan]] The "mantra of light" is the following: '''[[Sanskrit]]''' *Roman Script: Oṃ Amogha [[Vairocana]] [[Mahamudra|Mahāmudrā]] Maṇipadma Jvālapravarttaya Hūṃ *[[Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ]]: {{Script|Sidd|𑖌𑖼𑖀𑖦𑖺𑖑𑖪𑖹𑖨𑖺𑖓𑖡𑖦𑖮𑖯𑖦𑖲𑖟𑖿𑖨𑖯𑖦𑖜𑖰𑖢𑖟𑖿𑖦𑖕𑖿𑖪𑖯𑖩𑖢𑖿𑖨𑖪𑖨𑖿𑖝𑖿𑖝𑖧𑖮𑗝𑖽}} *[[Devanāgarī|Devanagari]]: ॐ अमोघ वैरोचन महामुद्रा मणिपद्म ज्वाल प्रवर्त्तय हूँ *{{Audio|Mantra_of_light.OGG|Sanskrit ''Pronunciation''}} '''[[Chinese language|Chinese script]]''' * 唵 阿謨伽 尾盧左曩 摩訶母捺囉 麽抳 鉢納麽 入嚩攞 鉢囉韈哆野 吽 * Ǎn ā mó jiā wěi lú zuǒ nǎng mó hē mǔ nà luō me nǐ bō nà me rù mó luó bō luō wà duō yě hōng '''[[Japanese language|Japanese]]''' * おん あぼきゃ べいろしゃのう まかぼだら まに はんどま じんばら はらばりたや うん * Romanized: Om abokya beiroshanō makabodara mani handoma jinbara harabaritaya un '''[[Korean language|Korean]]''' * 옴 아모가 바이로차나 마하무드라 마니 파드마 즈바라 프라바릍타야 훔 * Romanized: om amoga bairochana mahamudeura mani padeuma jeubara peurabareutaya hum '''[[Vietnamese language|Vietnamese]]''' * Án (Ông/Úm) a mộ già di lô tã nẵng, ma ha mẫu nại ra, na nĩ bát nạp mạ nhập phạ lã, nhập phạ lã, bác ra ni, mát đa dã hồng. '''[[Tibetan script|Tibetan]]''' * ཨོཾ་ཨ་མོ་གྷ་བཻ་རོ་ཙ་ན་མ་ཧཱ་མུ་དྲཱ་མ་ཎི་པདྨ​་ཛྭ་ལ་པྲ་ཝརྟཱ་ཡ་ཧཱུྃ === Translation === The translation of this mantra, according to Professor Mark Unno, is roughly: {{blockquote|Praise be to the flawless, all-pervasive illumination of the great [[mudra]] [or seal of the [[Buddha]] ]. Turn over to me the jewel, lotus and radiant light.}} Another translation according to the Dharmachakra Translation Committee is:
"Oṁ, amogha jewel-lotus of the splendorous great mudrā! Blaze! Set in motion! Hūṁ!"
== Use and history == [[File:Mandala of Mantra of Light.jpg|thumb|upright|right|Light Mantra Mandala]] The mantra appears in the ''Amoghapāśa-kalparāja-sūtra.'' It is used as a consecration mantra by the Buddhas of the ten directions which appear to ritually consecrate the bodhisattva Amoghapāśa. In the Tibetan version of this text, the passage which introduces the mantra is:
All the tathāgatas throughout the ten directions, the fully realized buddhas established throughout the three times, became clearly visible, appearing, through their magical power, to be suspended in the canopy of the sky. They applauded the blessed Tathāgata Śākyamuni: "Good! It is good, Śākyamuni! This king of consecrations is extremely difficult to obtain. The king of vidyās was also consecrated by the former tathāgatas established throughout the three times. All of us declare with a single voice that each of us will also consecrate the great king of vidyās, the Light of the Wish-Fulfilling Amogha Jewel." Subsequently, each of the tathāgatas established throughout the three times extended his golden hand and consecrated the king of vidyās by anointing his head. Each tathāgata thus bestowed the mudrā consecration upon the king of vidyās. The mantra for the mudrā consecration is: "Oṁ, amogha jewel-lotus of the splendorous great mudrā! Blaze! Set in motion! Hūṁ!" Immediately after bestowing the great mudrā of consecration, each tathāgata congratulated the king of vidyās and disappeared, returning to his particular buddha field.
Initially, the mantra received little mention in [[East Asian Buddhism|East Asian Buddhist]] texts. The mantra is found in a short text translated by [[Amoghavajra]] which focuses on its [[Apotropaic magic|apotropaic]] and healing uses (Taisho no. 1002, entitled ''Sutra of the Amoghapasa Light Mantra of the Buddha Vairocana’s Great Consecration'', 不空羂索毘盧遮那佛大灌頂光真言一卷). Although [[Kukai]] brought this sutra to Japan in the 9th century, there are no records that he ever utilized it in tantric practices. Records show gradually increasing use in the [[Heian Period]], until the 13th century when it was popularized in medieval [[Japanese Buddhism]] by [[Myoe|Myōe]], and later by [[Shingon]] monks [[Eison]] and [[Ninshō]] in their ministries.{{cite book | last = Tanabe Jr. | first = George | title = Religions of Japan in Practice | publisher = Princeton University Press | year = 1999 | isbn = 0-231-11286-6 | page=92}} Both the Mantra and the [[nembutsu]] were often incorporated by medieval Buddhists at one time or another, often in the same service. A common practice for the Mantra of Light was to sprinkle pure sand, blessed with this mantra, on the body of a deceased person or their tomb, based on teachings expounded in the Sutra. The belief was that a person who had accumulated much bad karma, and possible rebirth in [[Avici|Hell]] would be immediately freed and allowed a favorable rebirth into the [[Pure Land]] of Amitabha Buddha. This practice is known as {{nihongo|'''''dosha-kaji'''''|土砂加持}} in Japanese. Today, the mantra remains one of the most popular mantras in [[Shingon Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Buddhism topics}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Buddhist mantras]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Vairocana]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mantra of Light]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[en:Mantra of Light]]

It is also recited in Japanese [[Zen|Zen Buddhism]].Unno, Mark, ''Shingon Refractions: Myoe and the Mantra of Light,'' Ch. 1. The mantra is found in the ''Amoghapāśa-kalparāja-sūtra'' (Chinese translation at [[Taishō Tripiṭaka|Taisho]] no. 1092) as well as in the ''Sutra of the Mantra of the Unfailing Rope Snare of the Buddha Vairocana's Great Baptism'' (不空羂索毘盧遮那佛大灌頂光真言一卷, Taisho no. 1002) and is associated with the deity Amoghapāśa (lit. "Unfailing Rope"), a form of [[Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteshvara]]. It is also the mantra associated with the consecration (abhiseka) of Amoghapāśa by myriad Buddhas (hence its name as "mantra for the mudrā consecration" in the Tibetan version of the text)''.''{{cite book | last = Unno | first = Mark | title = Shingon Refractions: Myoe and the Mantra of Light | publisher = Wisdom Press | year = 2004 | isbn = 0-86171-390-7 | pages=1, 26–41}}{{Cite web |title=The Sovereign Ritual of Amoghapāśa {{!}} 84000 Reading Room |url=https://read.84000.co/translation/toh686.html?id=&part= |access-date=2022-12-06 |website=84000 Translating The Words of The Buddha |language=en}} == Mantra == [[file:Niutsuhimejinja 09.JPG|thumb|Stone stele of the Mantra of Light, Niutsuhime-jinja, Japan]] The "mantra of light" is the following: '''[[Sanskrit]]''' *Roman Script: Oṃ Amogha [[Vairocana]] [[Mahamudra|Mahāmudrā]] Maṇipadma Jvālapravarttaya Hūṃ *[[Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ]]: {{Script|Sidd|𑖌𑖼𑖀𑖦𑖺𑖑𑖪𑖹𑖨𑖺𑖓𑖡𑖦𑖮𑖯𑖦𑖲𑖟𑖿𑖨𑖯𑖦𑖜𑖰𑖢𑖟𑖿𑖦𑖕𑖿𑖪𑖯𑖩𑖢𑖿𑖨𑖪𑖨𑖿𑖝𑖿𑖝𑖧𑖮𑗝𑖽}} *[[Devanāgarī|Devanagari]]: ॐ अमोघ वैरोचन महामुद्रा मणिपद्म ज्वाल प्रवर्त्तय हूँ *{{Audio|Mantra_of_light.OGG|Sanskrit ''Pronunciation''}} '''[[Chinese language|Chinese script]]''' * 唵 阿謨伽 尾盧左曩 摩訶母捺囉 麽抳 鉢納麽 入嚩攞 鉢囉韈哆野 吽 * Ǎn ā mó jiā wěi lú zuǒ nǎng mó hē mǔ nà luō me nǐ bō nà me rù mó luó bō luō wà duō yě hōng '''[[Japanese language|Japanese]]''' * おん あぼきゃ べいろしゃのう まかぼだら まに はんどま じんばら はらばりたや うん * Romanized: Om abokya beiroshanō makabodara mani handoma jinbara harabaritaya un '''[[Korean language|Korean]]''' * 옴 아모가 바이로차나 마하무드라 마니 파드마 즈바라 프라바릍타야 훔 * Romanized: om amoga bairochana mahamudeura mani padeuma jeubara peurabareutaya hum '''[[Vietnamese language|Vietnamese]]''' * Án (Ông/Úm) a mộ già di lô tã nẵng, ma ha mẫu nại ra, na nĩ bát nạp mạ nhập phạ lã, nhập phạ lã, bác ra ni, mát đa dã hồng. '''[[Tibetan script|Tibetan]]''' * ཨོཾ་ཨ་མོ་གྷ་བཻ་རོ་ཙ་ན་མ་ཧཱ་མུ་དྲཱ་མ་ཎི་པདྨ​་ཛྭ་ལ་པྲ་ཝརྟཱ་ཡ་ཧཱུྃ === Translation === The translation of this mantra, according to Professor Mark Unno, is roughly: {{blockquote|Praise be to the flawless, all-pervasive illumination of the great [[mudra]] [or seal of the [[Buddha]] ]. A common practice for the Mantra of Light was to sprinkle pure sand, blessed with this mantra, on the body of a deceased person or their tomb, based on teachings expounded in the Sutra. The belief was that a person who had accumulated much bad karma, and possible rebirth in [[Avici|Hell]] would be immediately freed and allowed a favorable rebirth into the [[Pure Land]] of Amitabha Buddha. This practice is known as {{nihongo|'''''dosha-kaji'''''|土砂加持}} in Japanese. Today, the mantra remains one of the most popular mantras in [[Shingon Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Buddhism topics}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Buddhist mantras]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Vairocana]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mantra of Light]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:dardar]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[en:Mantra of Light]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Mantra in Japanese Buddhism}} {{Use dmy dates|date=August 2019}} The '''Mantra of Light''' ([[Japanese language|Japanese]]: ''kōmyō shingon'', 光明真言, Sanskrit: ''Prabhāsa-mantra''), also called the ''Mantra of the Light of Great Consecration'' (Ch: 大灌頂光真言) and ''Mantra of the Unfailing Rope Snare'', is an important [[mantra]] of the [[Shingon]] and [[Kegon]] sects of [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]]. It is also recited in Japanese [[Zen|Zen Buddhism]].Unno, Mark, ''Shingon Refractions: Myoe and the Mantra of Light,'' Ch. 1. The mantra is found in the ''Amoghapāśa-kalparāja-sūtra'' (Chinese translation at [[Taishō Tripiṭaka|Taisho]] no. 1092) as well as in the ''Sutra of the Mantra of the Unfailing Rope Snare of the Buddha Vairocana's Great Baptism'' (不空羂索毘盧遮那佛大灌頂光真言一卷, Taisho no. 1002) and is associated with the deity Amoghapāśa (lit. "Unfailing Rope"), a form of [[Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteshvara]]. It is also the mantra associated with the consecration (abhiseka) of Amoghapāśa by myriad Buddhas (hence its name as "mantra for the mudrā consecration" in the Tibetan version of the text)''.''{{cite book | last = Unno | first = Mark | title = Shingon Refractions: Myoe and the Mantra of Light | publisher = Wisdom Press | year = 2004 | isbn = 0-86171-390-7 | pages=1, 26–41}}{{Cite web |title=The Sovereign Ritual of Amoghapāśa {{!}} 84000 Reading Room |url=https://read.84000.co/translation/toh686.html?id=&part= |access-date=2022-12-06 |website=84000 Translating The Words of The Buddha |language=en}} == Mantra == [[file:Niutsuhimejinja 09.JPG|thumb|Stone stele of the Mantra of Light, Niutsuhime-jinja, Japan]] The "mantra of light" is the following: '''[[Sanskrit]]''' *Roman Script: Oṃ Amogha [[Vairocana]] [[Mahamudra|Mahāmudrā]] Maṇipadma Jvālapravarttaya Hūṃ *[[Siddhaṃ script|Siddhaṃ]]: {{Script|Sidd|𑖌𑖼𑖀𑖦𑖺𑖑𑖪𑖹𑖨𑖺𑖓𑖡𑖦𑖮𑖯𑖦𑖲𑖟𑖿𑖨𑖯𑖦𑖜𑖰𑖢𑖟𑖿𑖦𑖕𑖿𑖪𑖯𑖩𑖢𑖿𑖨𑖪𑖨𑖿𑖝𑖿𑖝𑖧𑖮𑗝𑖽}} *[[Devanāgarī|Devanagari]]: ॐ अमोघ वैरोचन महामुद्रा मणिपद्म ज्वाल प्रवर्त्तय हूँ *{{Audio|Mantra_of_light.OGG|Sanskrit ''Pronunciation''}} '''[[Chinese language|Chinese script]]''' * 唵 阿謨伽 尾盧左曩 摩訶母捺囉 麽抳 鉢納麽 入嚩攞 鉢囉韈哆野 吽 * Ǎn ā mó jiā wěi lú zuǒ nǎng mó hē mǔ nà luō me nǐ bō nà me rù mó luó bō luō wà duō yě hōng '''[[Japanese language|Japanese]]''' * おん あぼきゃ べいろしゃのう まかぼだら まに はんどま じんばら はらばりたや うん * Romanized: Om abokya beiroshanō makabodara mani handoma jinbara harabaritaya un '''[[Korean language|Korean]]''' * 옴 아모가 바이로차나 마하무드라 마니 파드마 즈바라 프라바릍타야 훔 * Romanized: om amoga bairochana mahamudeura mani padeuma jeubara peurabareutaya hum '''[[Vietnamese language|Vietnamese]]''' * Án (Ông/Úm) a mộ già di lô tã nẵng, ma ha mẫu nại ra, na nĩ bát nạp mạ nhập phạ lã, nhập phạ lã, bác ra ni, mát đa dã hồng. '''[[Tibetan script|Tibetan]]''' * ཨོཾ་ཨ་མོ་གྷ་བཻ་རོ་ཙ་ན་མ་ཧཱ་མུ་དྲཱ་མ་ཎི་པདྨ​་ཛྭ་ལ་པྲ་ཝརྟཱ་ཡ་ཧཱུྃ === Translation === The translation of this mantra, according to Professor Mark Unno, is roughly: {{blockquote|Praise be to the flawless, all-pervasive illumination of the great [[mudra]] [or seal of the [[Buddha]] ]. Turn over to me the jewel, lotus and radiant light.}} Another translation according to the Dharmachakra Translation Committee is:
"Oṁ, amogha jewel-lotus of the splendorous great mudrā! Blaze! Set in motion! Hūṁ!"
== Use and history == [[File:Mandala of Mantra of Light.jpg|thumb|upright|right|Light Mantra Mandala]] The mantra appears in the ''Amoghapāśa-kalparāja-sūtra.'' It is used as a consecration mantra by the Buddhas of the ten directions which appear to ritually consecrate the bodhisattva Amoghapāśa. In the Tibetan version of this text, the passage which introduces the mantra is:
All the tathāgatas throughout the ten directions, the fully realized buddhas established throughout the three times, became clearly visible, appearing, through their magical power, to be suspended in the canopy of the sky. They applauded the blessed Tathāgata Śākyamuni: "Good! It is good, Śākyamuni! This king of consecrations is extremely difficult to obtain. The king of vidyās was also consecrated by the former tathāgatas established throughout the three times. All of us declare with a single voice that each of us will also consecrate the great king of vidyās, the Light of the Wish-Fulfilling Amogha Jewel." Subsequently, each of the tathāgatas established throughout the three times extended his golden hand and consecrated the king of vidyās by anointing his head. Each tathāgata thus bestowed the mudrā consecration upon the king of vidyās. The mantra for the mudrā consecration is: "Oṁ, amogha jewel-lotus of the splendorous great mudrā! Blaze! Set in motion! Hūṁ!" Immediately after bestowing the great mudrā of consecration, each tathāgata congratulated the king of vidyās and disappeared, returning to his particular buddha field.
Initially, the mantra received little mention in [[East Asian Buddhism|East Asian Buddhist]] texts. The mantra is found in a short text translated by [[Amoghavajra]] which focuses on its [[Apotropaic magic|apotropaic]] and healing uses (Taisho no. 1002, entitled ''Sutra of the Amoghapasa Light Mantra of the Buddha Vairocana’s Great Consecration'', 不空羂索毘盧遮那佛大灌頂光真言一卷). Although [[Kukai]] brought this sutra to Japan in the 9th century, there are no records that he ever utilized it in tantric practices. Records show gradually increasing use in the [[Heian Period]], until the 13th century when it was popularized in medieval [[Japanese Buddhism]] by [[Myoe|Myōe]], and later by [[Shingon]] monks [[Eison]] and [[Ninshō]] in their ministries.{{cite book | last = Tanabe Jr. | first = George | title = Religions of Japan in Practice | publisher = Princeton University Press | year = 1999 | isbn = 0-231-11286-6 | page=92}} Both the Mantra and the [[nembutsu]] were often incorporated by medieval Buddhists at one time or another, often in the same service. A common practice for the Mantra of Light was to sprinkle pure sand, blessed with this mantra, on the body of a deceased person or their tomb, based on teachings expounded in the Sutra. The belief was that a person who had accumulated much bad karma, and possible rebirth in [[Avici|Hell]] would be immediately freed and allowed a favorable rebirth into the [[Pure Land]] of Amitabha Buddha. This practice is known as {{nihongo|'''''dosha-kaji'''''|土砂加持}} in Japanese. Today, the mantra remains one of the most popular mantras in [[Shingon Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Buddhism topics}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Buddhist mantras]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Vairocana]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mantra of Light]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:dardar]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[en:Mantra of Light]]
